title: Secrets and Codes - How Computers Keep Information Safe
description: Students learn encryption basics, practice encoding and decoding, and evaluate password strength and key security.
learningObjectives: Explain why encryption exists and what it protects.
Apply a simple cipher to encrypt and decrypt messages.
Describe what a key is and why secrecy of the key matters.
Describe what makes a password weak or strong with reasons.
prerequisites: Builds on Lesson 1 (algorithms), Lesson 3 (conditionals), and Lesson 4 (variables).
materials: Paper and pencils for each student
Optional one envelope per group for message passing
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain C (23A). Students explore ciphers, keys, and brute-force attacks through hands-on activities. Role model: Hedy Lamarr, co-inventor of frequency hopping, foundational to secure modern wireless communication.

teacherInstructions.setup: Keep vocabulary visible all lesson: Encryption, Cipher, Key, Decrypt, Hacker. Prepare Caesar-shift alphabet lines and message-passing setup.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question about WhatsApp message privacy and introduce encryption as scrambling with a key.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Crack-it warm-up with KHOOR, ZRUOG, FRGH and Caesar shift hinting.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro using message-plus-key model, password strength comparisons, and passphrase strategy.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Hacker intercept game with three rounds: public key failure, secret key success, and known-plaintext attack demonstration.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on key exchange, brute-force speed, and privacy versus safety debate.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete Exercise 1 (Encrypt and decrypt) and Exercise 2 (Password audit), then extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share custom ciphers and finish sentence: 'Encryption works because _____. '
discussionPrompts: Why did Round 1 fail and Round 2 improve in the intercept game?
How can two computers agree a secret key if a hacker hears everything?
Why is brute-force cracking essentially a loop that keeps updating guesses?
Should strong encryption ever be bypassed for safety investigations?
tips: Acknowledge end-to-end encryption vocabulary if students already know the phrase.
Use known-plaintext examples to show how predictable words weaken simple ciphers.
Emphasize that key secrecy is as important as algorithm choice.
During password ranking, steer toward evidence-based reasoning over opinions.
Connect brute-force attempts to loop and variable concepts from prior lessons.

teacherGuide: Core message: encryption transforms readable information into unreadable text unless you have the key. Students should experience why public shared keys fail and why secret per-message keys improve security. Push them to justify password strength using search space and predictability, not only symbols.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Encrypt and Decrypt
studentExercises.description: Complete three cipher tasks: encode with fixed shift, crack unknown shift, and design a stronger custom rule.
instructions: Task A: Encrypt DREAM SPACE IS FUN using Caesar shift 6. Expected encrypted text: JXKGS YVEGI OY LAT.
Task B: Crack YJQQT BTWQI with unknown shift between 1 and 25. Hint: common English greeting. Expected answer: HELLO WORLD with shift 17.
Task C: Design a better cipher that changes more than letter shift (for example adds fake letters or reorders words).
Write your Task C rule clearly so a partner can use it.
Decrypt your own Task A result back to the original message and fix any mismatch.
successCriteria: Task A encryption is correct and reversible.
Task B solved using systematic or known-plaintext reasoning.
Task C rule is unambiguous and harder than plain Caesar shift.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Password Audit
studentExercises.description: Rank six passwords from weakest to strongest and justify each ranking with evidence.
instructions: Rank these from weakest to strongest: password, 12345678, Angela2014, Tr0ub4dor!, correct horse battery staple, Xk#9mP$2qL.
Expected baseline ranking: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 5. Discuss why 4, 5, and 6 may still be debated.
Explain each ranking based on length, predictability, and character variety.
Identify why names and years are guessed early in attacks.
Write one improved replacement for the weakest password.
successCriteria: Pair gives reasoned ranking, not guesses.
Pair identifies that length can outperform symbol-heavy short passwords.
At least one replacement shows stronger structure and memorability.

studentExercises.title: Extension - Build an Unbreakable System
studentExercises.description: Design and test a two-layer encryption method, then challenge another student to crack it without the rule.
instructions: Create a two-step cipher system (for example reverse message then apply Caesar shift).
Encrypt a short message with your system.
Pass only encrypted message to another student; keep the rule secret.
After 2 minutes, reveal rule and compare cracking strategies.
Answer challenge prompts about key-space size and why one-way functions differ from your method.
successCriteria: Encrypted message can be correctly decrypted with the rule.
Student explains why two layers increase cracking effort.

studentChallenges.title: Hacker Intercept Game
studentChallenges.description: Send and protect 3-word secret messages while an interceptor tries to crack them in real time.
instructions: Round 1: all groups use Caesar +4 with public key. Observe why all messages are readable.
Round 2: each group chooses private shift 1-25 and shares only with target team.
Pass encrypted 3-word messages through teacher interceptor and attempt decryption.
Compare success rates between public and secret-key rounds.
Identify one improvement that would make interception even harder.
successCriteria: Group encrypts and decrypts correctly with chosen key.
Group can explain why public key-on-board setup fails.
At least one concrete hardening strategy is proposed.

studentChallenges.title: Known-Plaintext Defense Lab
studentChallenges.description: Use a known opening word to break one message, then redesign message format to resist this attack.
instructions: Assume first word is a common term like meet or the and derive likely shift.
Decrypt the rest of the message with derived key.
Redesign the sender rule to avoid predictable starts.
Add one anti-pattern trick (padding, word reorder, or key rotation).
Retest whether your modified message is harder to crack in 60 seconds.
successCriteria: Pair demonstrates at least one successful known-plaintext break.
Defense redesign removes simple predictable opening weakness.
